metadata.summary: US state/local government compliance: WCAG 2.1 AA by April 2026 (50K+ pop) or 2027. Full scope, remediation roadmap, VPAT documentation, training, procurement standards.

content.title: ADA Title II Compliance 2026
content.summary: Compliance methodology for US state and local government entities required to meet WCAG 2.1 Level AA under the DOJ ADA Title II final rule: large entities (50K+ population) by April 24, 2026; smaller entities by April 24, 2027. Covers full scope (web, mobile apps, multimedia, PDFs, third-party content), the six-step remediation roadmap, accessibility statement requirements, VPAT/ACR documentation, training, and procurement standards.

why.p: Non-compliance risks DOJ investigation, private lawsuits, loss of federal funding, and multi-year consent decrees. The scope is broader than HTML: mobile apps, PDFs, video captions, interactive forms, and third-party social embeds are all in scope. Overlay tools (accessibility.widget) do not constitute compliance — the DOJ has explicitly warned against them. VPAT 2.5 INT alone is not proof of conformance; courts require real test evidence.

list.item: US state/local government entity or federal-funded program building or auditing public-facing digital services.
Vendor responding to government RFP requiring VPAT/ACR and remediation plan.
Procurement officer evaluating third-party SaaS for accessibility risk before contract award.
Pre-litigation triage after a DOJ complaint or Title II demand letter.
Drafting or reviewing accessibility statements, procurement clauses, or staff training plans.
Private commercial sites unrelated to government funding — ADA Title III and WCAG 2.2 AA apply; different case law.
EU-only products — use regulatory-compliance-2026 for EAA + EN 301 549.
Internal-only tools not used by the public — Section 504/508 may apply instead of the Title II web rule.
Greenfield design — apply accessibility-first-design from day one to avoid remediation cost.

section.name: compliance-deadlines-and-scope
section.p: The DOJ ADA Title II final rule sets specific deadlines and defines scope broadly beyond just websites.

rule.statement: Large state/local government entities (serving 50,000+ population) must comply by April 24, 2026; smaller entities by April 24, 2027. Standard: WCAG 2.1 Level AA.
rule.rationale: DOJ Final Rule published April 2024. ADA Title II applies to all state and local government entities that receive federal assistance — not just websites, but all digital services used by the public.

list.item: Public-facing websites and web applications.
Mobile applications (iOS and Android) — requires platform-specific AT testing, not just web axe scans.
Multimedia content: all pre-recorded video requires captions; all live video requires real-time captions or equivalent alternative.
PDF documents and interactive forms.
Third-party content embedded on government sites (social media embeds, widgets, chat).
Online services and citizen portals.

section.name: wcag-21-aa-requirements-by-principle
section.p: WCAG 2.1 is organized into four principles. Here are the key requirements for each:

example.code: Principle | Key Requirements Perceivable | Alt text (1.1.1), captions (1.2.x), contrast 4.5:1 normal / 3:1 large (1.4.3), responsive (1.4.4) Operable | Keyboard access (2.1.1), no keyboard traps (2.1.2), no time limits or adjustable (2.2.1), skip links (2.4.1), focus visible (2.4.7) Understandable | Language attribute (3.1.1), predictable navigation (3.2), error identification (3.3.1), error suggestion (3.3.3) Robust | Valid HTML with name/role/value (4.1.2), status messages (4.1.3), compatible with AT

section.name: video-and-document-requirements
section.p: Video and PDF accessibility are the most-litigated gaps in government compliance. These requirements are non-negotiable.

rule.statement: All pre-recorded government video must have accurate, synchronized captions and audio descriptions before publication — not retroactively after a complaint.
rule.rationale: Courts define "accurate" as >= 99% accuracy; auto-generated captions from Whisper or YouTube are ~92-96% accurate and require human QA before claiming compliance.

list.item: Captions: accurate, synchronized, complete; include speaker identification and non-speech sounds.
Audio descriptions: visual information not available in the soundtrack must be described (extended audio descriptions if pauses are insufficient).
PDFs: tagged PDF structure, defined reading order, alt text for images, labeled form fields, bookmarks for documents over 10 pages.
Media player: keyboard accessible controls; screen reader compatible; caption and audio description toggle.

section.name: exemptions
section.p: Exemptions are narrow, individually documented, and must be accompanied by accessible alternatives.

rule.statement: Exemptions are narrow and individually documented; each must be accompanied by an accessible alternative access method.
rule.rationale: DOJ has rejected broad exemptions ("everything before the deadline"). Once an exemption applies, the organization must still provide alternative access to the information or service.

list.item: Pre-effective-date content: content published before the compliance date and NOT subsequently altered. Altering old content revokes the exemption.
Archived content: clearly labeled as archived, not updated, not actively relied on by the public.
Third-party content not under government control: must still provide an accessible alternative.
Undue burden: requires documented cost analysis specific to the entity — not a general "too expensive" claim.
Fundamental alteration: changes the essential nature of the service; very rarely applies to digital accessibility.

antipattern.description: Deploying an accessibility overlay widget as the primary compliance strategy.
antipattern.reason: The DOJ has explicitly warned that overlays do not substitute for underlying code fixes. They remain legally exposed to Title II complaints. Fix the source, not the surface.

section.name: six-step-remediation-roadmap
section.p: A structured approach from audit through ongoing monitoring is required for government compliance.

rule.statement: Track conformance per page per WCAG criterion in a matrix; do not report a single global score — DOJ guidance asks for criterion-level evidence.
rule.rationale: A single percentage score cannot demonstrate that specific legal requirements are met. Criterion-level tracking is required for VPAT 2.5 and for responding to DOJ data requests.

example.code:
Step 1: Audit - Automated scan (axe, WAVE, Lighthouse) on all public pages - Manual keyboard and screen reader testing on key user flows - PDF and video accessibility audit - Mobile app testing (XCUITest + Accessibility Inspector, Espresso AccessibilityChecks)
Step 2: Gap Analysis - Map findings to WCAG 2.1 AA criteria - Identify pages/flows with highest traffic and severity - Inventory pre-effective-date vs. post-effective-date content
Step 3: Remediation Roadmap - Critical (blocks access): immediate / hotfix - High (high-traffic pages): 0-3 months - Medium: 3-6 months - Low: 6-12 months
Step 4: Implementation - Fix in priority order; test fixes before deploying - Caption all new video before publication - Remediate PDFs with CommonLook or Adobe Acrobat Pro
Step 5: Ongoing Monitoring - Automated axe scan in CI/CD on every PR - Quarterly manual audits - Annual comprehensive audit with AT testing - Accessibility statement updated within 12 months
Step 6: Conformance Documentation - VPAT 2.5 INT (covers Section 508 + WCAG 2.1 + EN 301 549) - Accessibility Conformance Report (ACR) from test evidence - Public accessibility statement published - Feedback mechanism live and monitored

section.name: accessibility-statement-requirements
section.p: A public-facing accessibility statement is effectively required under the DOJ Title II rule.

rule.statement: Publish an accessibility statement on every government website; it must include conformance level, known limitations, a feedback contact, and a date of last review.
rule.rationale: The DOJ Title II rule effectively requires an accessibility statement. A statement without a current review date (within 12 months) or a working contact address is itself a compliance gap.

list.item: Organizational commitment to accessibility.
Conformance status: "Partially conformant with WCAG 2.1 Level AA" if any known issues exist.
Known limitations listed individually — vague "ongoing effort" is not sufficient.
Contact: email and phone for accessibility issues; response within 2 business days.
Date of last review — must be within 12 months.
Escalation path (e.g., DOJ ADA Information Line) for unresolved complaints.

section.name: training-and-procurement
section.p: Building accessibility into the organization requires training and procurement policies.

list.item: Training required for: content creators/editors, web developers, procurement officers, customer service, leadership.
Training topics: WCAG 2.1 AA requirements, accessible document creation, screen reader testing basics, accessible form design, video captioning and audio descriptions.
Procurement: require VPAT 2.5 INT from all software/SaaS vendors; include remediation timeline and reaudit-on-major-version clauses in contracts.
Caption all videos before posting to social media; provide alt text for all images; choose accessible social platforms.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Agents excel at several compliance tasks but do not replace legal review or human testing.

Agents excel at: WCAG-criterion mapping of axe/Pa11y findings, drafting accessibility statements, generating VPAT/ACR boilerplate, building remediation roadmap from issue tracker, monitoring CI for regressions. Agents do NOT replace: legal review, real screen-reader user testing, complex video audio description writing, undue-burden analysis. Two-track pipeline: technical agent (audit + fix) + compliance agent (VPAT, statements, contract clauses).

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — model each WCAG criterion gap as an SDD task with criterion ID and conformance evidence requirement.
Compliance-doc subagent — converts audit JSON into VPAT 2.5 INT (Revised Section 508 + WCAG 2.1) markdown.
Procurement subagent — extracts a11y clauses from a vendor's VPAT, flags non-conformant criteria.
See also: a11y-testing (technical scan side) and wcag-22-compliance (criterion details).

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.p: These prompt patterns guide agents in government compliance tasks:

example.title: ADA Title II conformance audit
example.code: You are an ADA Title II compliance agent. Given the audit JSON (axe + Pa11y + manual screen-reader notes), produce: 1) WCAG 2.1 AA conformance table (Supports / Partially / Does Not / N/A) per criterion. 2) Remediation roadmap: Critical (immediate) / High (3mo) / Med (6mo) / Low (12mo). 3) Draft accessibility statement using DOJ-aligned template. Cite the WCAG criterion number for every entry. Never assert "Supports" for a criterion not directly tested.

example.title: Vendor VPAT procurement review
example.code: Review this vendor VPAT 2.5 for procurement. List criteria marked "Does Not Support" or "Partially Supports", their user impact, and required contract clauses (remediation timeline, escrow, periodic reaudit).

list.item: @axe-core/cli — WCAG 2.1/2.2 AA scanner. Install: npm i -g @axe-core/cli
pa11y-ci — Sitemap-driven scan with thresholds. Install: npm i -g pa11y-ci
lighthouse — Auditing per page. Install: npm i -g lighthouse
pdfix-pdf-accessibility — PDF/UA validation, remediation. https://pdfix.net
pac-2024 (PAC) — Free PDF accessibility checker. https://pdfua.foundation
andi (bookmarklet) — Manual ANDI tool from SSA. https://www.ssa.gov/accessibility/andi/
ffmpeg + whisper — Auto-caption pipeline (still requires human QA). https://github.com/openai/whisper
accessibility-insights-cli — MS automated tab-stop and guided checks. https://accessibilityinsights.io

section.name: services-and-apps
section.p: Various SaaS platforms and services support government compliance at scale:

list.item: Siteimprove (SaaS) — REST API. Used widely by US gov; tracks compliance posture, exports gov-style reports.
Level Access / AudioEye (SaaS) — API + manual audit. Combine automated + human; AudioEye's overlay alone is NOT compliance — DOJ has stated overlays don't substitute for fixes.
Deque axe Auditor + axe Monitor (SaaS) — API. Continuous monitoring, IGT, regression tracking.
3PlayMedia (SaaS) — API. Captioning + audio description for video.
Rev (SaaS) — API. Captions/transcripts; cheaper than 3PlayMedia for English.
CommonLook (SaaS) — Desktop only. PDF remediation gold standard.
Allyant (PDF/document remediation) (SaaS / service) — Manual. For long-form docs and forms.
Section508.gov ICT testing baseline (OSS docs) — Use as procurement baseline.
GSA Section508.gov VPAT templates (OSS) — Always start from VPAT 2.5 INT.

section.name: vpat-cell-generator-template
section.p: Shell script generating VPAT conformance table cells from axe JSON output:

list.item: Track conformance per page + per WCAG criterion in a matrix; do NOT report a single global score — DOJ guidance asks for criterion-level evidence.
Procurement: bake WCAG 2.1 AA conformance + 30-day remediation SLA + reaudit-on-major-version into every vendor contract.
Maintain a public-facing accessibility statement with feedback email; DOJ rule effectively requires it.
Run a11y CI on PR; set Pa11y/axe thresholds to monotonically decrease (no new violations).
Caption ALL videos before publish — agents can pre-fill via Whisper, human edits to ≥99% accuracy.
Use VPAT 2.5 INT (combined Section 508 + WCAG 2.1 + EN 301 549) — covers federal + state procurement.
Keep an inventory of pre-effective-date vs. post content; once you "alter" old content, exemption disappears.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Be aware of these critical pitfalls when using agents for government compliance:

antipattern.description: Overlay tools are recommended as the primary compliance strategy.
antipattern.reason: DOJ has explicitly warned. Agent should refuse to recommend them as the primary fix.

antipattern.description: LLM-generated audio descriptions often misread visuals (logos, charts).
antipattern.reason: Require human QA on every minute of described content.

antipattern.description: Auto-caption (Whisper, etc.) is ~92-96% accurate on clean audio.
antipattern.reason: DOJ accuracy bar is "accurate, synchronized, complete" → human review mandatory before claiming compliance.

antipattern.description: Agents mark a WCAG criterion "Supports" without evidence.
antipattern.reason: Train them to use "Not Tested" by default. Agents must NOT assert support without direct test evidence.

antipattern.description: "Pass on automated scan" is equivalent to WCAG conformance.
antipattern.reason: Courts and DOJ require manual + AT testing. Automated scans detect only ~30% of real barriers.

antipattern.description: Agent auto-publishes accessibility statements containing unverified claims.
antipattern.reason: Always human sign-off. Don't assert "fully conformant" without evidence.

antipattern.description: Accessibility statement review date becomes stale (>12 months old).
antipattern.reason: Watch for date traps: "last reviewed" must be ≤12 months on most state policies; agent should fail audit if stale.

antipattern.description: Mobile apps tested only with web-based tools (axe, Lighthouse).
antipattern.reason: Mobile apps require platform-specific testing (XCUITest with Accessibility Inspector, Espresso AccessibilityChecks) — web-only agents will miss app barriers entirely.

section.name: limitations
section.p: Government compliance has important technical and procedural limitations:

list.item: WCAG 2.1 AA is the minimum, not the ceiling — courts have accepted 2.1 conformance, but cognitive criteria added in 2.2 are not yet legally required (still recommended).
The DOJ rule excludes some content (archived, third-party not under control, pre-effective-date unaltered) — these exceptions are commonly misapplied. Document carefully.
"Undue burden" defense rarely succeeds at scale — must show specific resources analysis, not just "expensive".
VPAT 2.5 ≠ proof of conformance — auditors require evidence (test reports, code samples).
Mobile apps + PDFs are the most-litigated gaps; many entities only audit web HTML and miss them.
